title: Filling a large plasterboard hole with a patch plate
subtitle: Repair a hole bigger than 50 mm in a plasterboard wall cleanly
excerpt: How to repair a large hole in a plasterboard (drywall) wall using a proprietary metal patch plate or a timber-backed patch. Covers cutting the hole square, fitting the backing, patching with jointing compound, and feathering for an invisible repair.
sourceNotes: British Gypsum White Book (plasterboard repair methods). British Standard BS 8212: Code of practice for dry lining and partitioning. Collins Complete DIY Manual, Albert Jackson and David Day (1989).

glossaryTerms.definition: A premixed plaster-based filler used to tape and finish plasterboard joints. Available as a setting compound (chemical set, fast) or a drying compound (air dry, slow). For patch repairs, use an all-purpose drying compound in a tub: it has a long open time and can be applied in thin coats. Shrinks slightly as it dries, so several thin coats beat one thick one.

The technique of spreading a thin coat of filler or jointing compound outward from a repair at a very shallow angle, reducing the coat to near-zero thickness at the outer edge. A feathered edge is invisible under paint; a sharp or abrupt edge creates a visible halo around the repair.

content.text: Holes between 50 mm and 150 mm in a plasterboard wall (left by a door handle, a pipe chase, or a fist in frustration) cannot be filled with a standard tube filler. The gap has nothing to bridge onto. A proprietary metal patch plate (an aluminium plate with a self-adhesive mesh face) provides an instant rigid backing. A timber-backed insert cut from spare plasterboard is the alternative for larger holes. Both methods use jointing compound built up in thin coats and

A proprietary metal patch plate (sold in hardware shops to cover holes up to 100 to 150 mm; choose one larger than the hole). All-purpose jointing compound in a tub. Scrim mesh tape. 120-grit and 180-grit sanding paper. The utility knife. A 50 mm filling knife and a wider 100 mm filling knife. A damp sponge.

Cut the hole to a clean shape with the utility knife. A square or rectangular hole is easier to patch than a ragged tear. Score the plasterboard with the knife and snap the waste back; the paper face will tear cleanly if you score along a steel straight-edge. Remove all loose or crumbling plasterboard back to the solid edge. The hole now has clean, hard edges for the patch plate to grip.

Peel the backing from the patch plate and centre it over the hole, pressing the self-adhesive mesh firmly onto the surrounding plasterboard. The metal plate bridges the hole; the mesh extends onto the solid wall on all sides, giving the compound something to key into. If the hole is too large for a single patch plate, use two overlapping plates.
Step 3, apply the first coat of jointing compound

to a smooth, peanut-butter consistency (tub compound is often used straight from the tub). Apply the first coat with the 50 mm knife, pressing firmly into the mesh and the hole, but keeping the coat thin; no more than 3 mm. The aim of this first coat is to embed the mesh and fill the hole to roughly level, not to produce a flat surface. Allow to dry completely; jointing compound dries from white to cream; do not rush this stage.
Step 4, build up and feather outward

Apply the second coat with the wider 100 mm knife, spreading it thinly across the patch and extending it 50 mm beyond the edges of the mesh in all directions. This wider coat starts the feathering process. Allow to dry. Apply a third coat, feathering the edges out a further 50 mm and reducing the thickness at the outer edge to near zero. Three thin coats produce a more invisible repair than one thick coat that cracks as it shrinks.

Once the final coat is fully dry, sand lightly with 120-grit paper, then finish with 180-grit. Wipe off dust with a damp cloth. Apply a coat of diluted PVA (1 part to 5 parts water) or a proprietary plaster primer to the patched area to seal it before painting. Without priming, the repaired area will absorb the first coat of emulsion unevenly and show as a dull patch (a suction shadow) under the finished paint.

attrs.symptom: Compound shrinks and cracks as it dries
attrs.cause: Coat applied too thick in a single pass.
attrs.fix: Multiple thin coats (max 3 mm each) prevent shrinkage cracking. Let each coat dry fully before the next.

attrs.symptom: Patch visible under paint as a slight halo
attrs.cause: Feathering not taken far enough out from the repair; sharp transition at the edge of the compound.
attrs.fix: Wet-sand the edges of the halo to blend them further into the surrounding wall, prime again, and repaint.
